calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion For Early Discovery And Related Orders
Off Calendar Apr-09-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR ORDER AUTHORIZING EARLY DISCOVERY AND RELATED ORDERS

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Santiago Ampudia Castelazo's motion for early discovery is granted as stated herein.

Plaintiff moves under Code of Civil Procedure, sec. 2025.210 for leave to serve business records subpoenas on several social media platforms used to publish allegedly defamatory material. Subdivision (b) of sec. 2025.210 reads: "The plaintiff may serve a deposition notice without leave of court on any date that is 20 days after the service of the summons on, or appearance by, any defendant. On motion with or without notice, the court, for good cause shown, may grant to a plaintiff leave to serve a deposition notice on an earlier date." The rule applies to third-party deposition notices for production of business records. (See California Shellfish Inc. v. United Shellfish Co. (1997) 56 Cal.App.4th 16, 21.) A plaintiff seeking early discovery to identify an anonymous defendant must: a) attempt to notify the anonymous defendant of the request, and b) make a prima facie showing of the elements of his/her claim(s). (Krinsky v. Doe 6 (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1154, 1171-1172; see also Glassdoor, Inc. v. Superior Court (2017) 9 Cal.App.5th 623, 634-35.) The purpose of this requirement is to weigh the First Amendment right of internet speakers to speak anonymously against the plaintiff's interest in discovery the speaker's identity in order to pursue claims against the speaker. (Krinsky, supra, 159 Cal.App.4th, at p. 1165.)

To establish a claim for defamation, a plaintiff must show: a) a publication, b) that is false, c) that is defamatory, d) that is unprivileged, and e) that has a natural tendency to injure or to cause special damage. (See Price v. Operating Engineers Loc. Union No. 3 (2011) 195 Cal.App.4th 962, 970.) Plaintiff, a private figure, has made a prima facie showing supporting his defamation claim. (Ampudia Castelazo Decl., pars. 24, 28, 72, 86, 90-92, 93.) Unlike the plaintiff in Krinsky who sought to learn the speakers of opinion and mixed opinion, Plaintiff here points to assertions of fact with a tendency to harm. (See, e.g., Sanders v. Walsh (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 855, 864 [online claim that plaintiff engaged in specifically identified acts of perjury and fraud was an assertion of fact].) The court also finds that Plaintiff has made a reasonable attempt to notify the anonymous defendants of his early discovery request. (Vulic Decl., pars. 2-26 & Exs. 1-17.)

Thus, Plaintiff's request for authorization to conduct early discovery under Code of Civil Procedure, sec. 2025.210 and related orders is granted. Plaintiff is permitted to engage in early discovery for the limited purpose of identifying Defendant Does by serving the subpoenas attached as Exhibits 18-27 to the Declaration of Leah Rosa Vulic in support of Plaintiff's Motion. The subpoenaed service providers are ordered to give notice to their customers whose material is the subject of the subpoenas. Plaintiff is ordered to provide a copy of this order with all subpoenas served pursuant to it.

Good cause appearing, this order is an order under 47 U.S. Code, sec. 551, subdivision (c), authorizing a cable operator that receives a subpoena from Plaintiff to disclose personally identifiable information about its subscribers.

The court finds Plaintiff has exercised due diligence in attempting to notify Does of his motion. Good cause appearing, to the extent any recipient of a subpoena authorized by this order is a telephone corporation which is a public utility or otherwise possesses personal records subject to Code of Civil Procedure sec. 1985.3, the notice requirements of sec. 1985.3 are hereby waived pursuant to sec. 1985.3, subdivision (h).

Proposed order submitted. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)
